1: Josiah celebrates the Passover to YHWH in Jerusalem — wayyišḥaṭ happesaḥ bᵉʾarbāʿāh ʿāśār laḥōḏeš hārīʾšôn — on the fourteenth of the first month. The precision of the date matches the Mosaic prescription exactly (Exodus 12:6; Leviticus 23:5; Numbers 9:3). After all of Josiah’s reform work — the discovery of the Law, the covenant renewal, the destruction of high places — the Passover is the covenant feast that seals the reform. The Passover re-enacted is the nation’s confession that its identity as YHWH’s people is grounded in the Exodus event.
2: Josiah stations the priests at their posts (wayyaʿămiḏ hakḵōhănîm ʿal-mišmᵉrôtām) and encourages them (wayyḥazzēq — strengthens/encourages) for the service of YHWH’s house. The verb ḥāzaq (to strengthen) is the charge-to-Joshua word (Joshua 1:6-7; Deuteronomy 31:7) — Josiah is commissioning the priests with the same urgency as the conquest commission. Priestly service is a form of spiritual warfare requiring the same courage.
3: Josiah speaks to the Levites who taught all Israel and who were holy to YHWH: tᵉnû ʾet-ʾărôn haqqōḏeš babbayit ʾăšer bānāh Šᵉlōmōh ʾên-lāḵem māśāʾ baḵḵātēp — “Put the holy ark in the house that Solomon the son of David built. You need not carry it on your shoulders any more.” The Levites’ ancient transport duty — carrying the ark on their shoulders during the wilderness (Numbers 7:9; 2 Chronicles 5:4) — is now permanently obsolete. The ark is in its permanent home; the Levites are freed for ministry to the people (ʿiḇᵉḏû ʾet-YHWH ʾĕlōhêḵem wᵉʾet-ʿammô Yiśrāʾēl).
4: Josiah commands the Levites to prepare themselves by their fathers’ houses, by their divisions, following the written instructions of David and Solomon. The dual appeal to David’s miktāḇ (written order) and Solomon’s miktāḇ (written document) establishes the documentary authority behind the liturgical organization. The Chronicler consistently grounds temple service in written, transmitted order — not improvised piety but the disciplined form that the fathers established.
5: The Levites are to stand in the holy place by the divisions of the fathers’ houses of your brothers the laypeople — a distribution of Levites throughout the congregation. Uḥᵉlōqat bêt-āḇ (a division of the father’s house) — each lay division has a corresponding Levitical assignment, ensuring that no one in the gathered community is without priestly support during the Passover. The structured pastoral deployment reflects the Chronicler’s vision of the whole people cared for by the whole Levitical corps.
6: ûšaḥăṭû happeseḥ wᵉhitqaḏḏᵉšû wᵉhāḵînû laʾăḥêḵem laʿăśôt kiḏᵉḇar YHWH bᵉyaḏ-Mōšeh — “And slaughter the Passover lamb and consecrate yourselves and prepare for your brothers to act according to the word of YHWH given through Moses.” Three imperatives — slaughter, consecrate, prepare — structure the Levitical mission. The authority of Moses’ word (kiḏᵉḇar YHWH bᵉyaḏ-Mōšeh) frames the Passover as the fulfillment of Torah instruction, not creative innovation.
7: Josiah gives from his own property 30,000 young goats and kids and 3,000 bulls for the Passover offerings. The king’s personal donation from his possessions — the royal sᵉgullāh — echoes David’s personal donation for the temple (1 Chronicles 29:3-5). The reforming king who funds the people’s worship from his own treasury models the principle that leadership gives first and most generously before calling others to give.
8: The princes and officials give willingly (lᵉnᵉḏāḇāh) — Hilkiah, Zechariah, Jehiel give 2,600 Passover offerings from the priests; Conaniah and his brothers give 5,000 Passover offerings among the Levites. The freewill giving of the leaders (nᵉḏāḇāh) echoes the tabernacle and temple-dedication offerings — the same vocabulary of voluntary generosity that the Chronicler uses for the highest expressions of covenant devotion.
9: Conaniah and Shemaiah and Nethanel his brothers, and Hashabiah and Jeiel and Jozabad, chiefs of the Levites, gave to the Levites for the Passover offerings 5,000 lambs and goats and 500 bulls. The sheer quantity of animals — overwhelming abundance beyond necessity — signals that this is not a minimal compliance Passover but a feast of extravagant covenant devotion. The abundance of the sacrifice mirrors the abundance of YHWH’s original deliverance from Egypt.
10: The service is prepared (wattikkôn hāʿăḇōḏāh): the priests stand at their places, the Levites in their divisions according to the king’s command. Wattikkôn (was established/prepared) is the key verb — the entire service infrastructure is in place before any offering is made. The Chronicler’s ideal is a worship order that runs because it is properly structured, not because of last-minute scrambling.
11: The Passover animals are slaughtered; the priests sprinkle the blood (wayyizrᵉqû hakḵōhănîm) from their hand, and the Levites flay (ûmapšîṭîm) them. The technical division of labor — priests handle the blood (always a priestly prerogative, as blood is the life, Leviticus 17:11), Levites flay — is the precision of covenant liturgy. Every detail has its appointed handler; the system of accountability extends to each physical action in the sacrifice.
12: They set aside the burnt offerings to give them to the groupings of the laypeople by fathers’ houses, to offer to YHWH as it is written in the Book of Moses. The phrase kakkātûb bᵉsēper Mōšeh (as it is written in the Book of Moses) appears here and in v6 — the double reference grounds the entire Passover in textual authority. Josiah’s great Passover is the king’s obedience to the recovered Torah, whose authority is not royal decree but divine word through Moses.
13: wayyᵉḇaššᵉlû happesaḥ bāʾēš kammišpāṭ — “They roasted the Passover lamb with fire according to the rule.” The fire-roasting (bayyebalûšēl bāʾēš) follows the Mosaic prescription exactly (Exodus 12:8-9: “roasted over fire” — not boiled). The kammišpāṭ (according to the rule/ordinance) applies to the Passover; the other holy offerings (qōḏāšîm) they boil in pots and caldrons. The precision of fire-vs-boil reflects the Chronicler’s concern for exact liturgical compliance.
14: Afterward the Levites prepare for themselves and for the priests, because the priests, the sons of Aaron, were busy until night offering the burnt offerings and fat portions. The Levites’ self-preparation comes last — they serve others first, eat after the community is served. The ethic of servant leadership embedded in the liturgical order: those who serve the service eat after those they serve. The Chronicler’s ideal of Levitical ministry is other-centered before self-caring.
15: The singers, sons of Asaph, are at their station according to the commandment of David, and Asaph and Heman and Jeduthun the king’s seer. The temple singers occupy their posts throughout the Passover — they do not rotate to help with the animal processing but maintain continuous song. The gatekeepers are at each gate. Neither singers nor gatekeepers leave their appointed stations: kî-ʾaḥêhem halᵉwiyyim hēḵînû lāhem — because their Levite brothers prepared for them.
16: wattikkôn kol-ʿăḇōḏat YHWH bayyôm hahûʾ — “So all the service of the LORD was prepared that day.” The summary verdict: the entire liturgical system operated as designed. The word kûn (to be established/prepared) appears at v10 (preparations) and here (completion) — bookending the Passover execution. What was established at the beginning was realized at the end: ordered service, faithfully executed, becomes the completion of what it began.
17: The people of Israel who were present kept the Passover at that time and the Feast of Unleavened Bread for seven days. The full eight-day festival — Passover plus the week of Unleavened Bread — is observed completely. Ûḥag hammaṣṣôt šiḇʿat yāmîm — the seven days of the Feast of Unleavened Bread complete the festival unit that the Torah mandates (Exodus 12:15-20; Leviticus 23:6-8). Josiah’s Passover is not abbreviated or curtailed by administrative convenience.
18: wᵉlōʾ-naʿăśāh ḵappesaḥ hazzeh bᵉYiśrāʾēl mimê Šᵉmûʾēl hannāḇîʾ ûḵol malḵê Yiśrāʾēl lōʾ ʿāśû ḵappesaḥ kaʾăšer ʿāśāh Yōšiyyāhû — “No Passover like it had been kept in Israel since the days of Samuel the prophet. None of the kings of Israel had kept such a Passover as was kept by Josiah.” The superlative is remarkable in its historical reach: back to Samuel, bypassing David and Solomon. The reference to Samuel (rather than Moses or Joshua) recalls that Samuel was the last judge who restored proper covenant worship before the monarchy — Josiah’s Passover connects pre-monarchic covenant faithfulness to the final reforming king.
19: This Passover was kept in the eighteenth year of Josiah’s reign. The year-marker connects this celebration to the discovery of the Law book (2 Chronicles 34:8) and Josiah’s covenant renewal (2 Chronicles 34:31-33) — the Passover is the liturgical climax of the reform year. The internal chronological consistency is the Chronicler’s way of showing that the discovery of Torah, the renewal of covenant, and the celebration of Passover are a single integrated event: hearing leads to committing leads to celebrating.
20: After all this, when Josiah had prepared the temple, Neco king of Egypt came up to fight at Carchemish on the Euphrates, and Josiah went out against him. The temporal marker ʾaḥărê kol-zōʾt (after all this) sets the Passover and the battle in stark contrast: the height of covenant celebration is immediately followed by Josiah’s inexplicable military engagement. Carchemish is far from Judah’s territory — Josiah has no strategic reason to engage Neco there; his motivation is never stated by the Chronicler.
21: Neco sends messengers to Josiah: meh-lî wᵉlāḵ melek Yᵉhûḏāh lōʾ ʿāleḵā ʾattāh hayyôm kî ʾel-bêt milḥamtî — “What have I to do with you, king of Judah? I am not against you this day, but against the house with which I am at war. And God has commanded me to hurry. Cease opposing God, who is with me, lest he destroy you.” The formula mah-lî wᵉlāḵ (what have I to do with you?) is a rebuff-formula in biblical Hebrew (Judges 11:12; 2 Samuel 16:10; 1 Kings 17:18). Neco claims divine authorization — and the Chronicler implies he speaks truth (v22: Josiah “did not listen to the words of Neco from the mouth of God”).
22: wᵉlōʾ hēsîḇ Yōšiyyāhû ʾet-pānāyw mimmennû wayyithappēś lᵉhillāḥēm bô wᵉlōʾ šāmaʿ ʾel-diḇrê Nᵉkôh mippî ʾĕlōhîm — “Josiah did not turn away from him, but disguised himself to fight with him. He did not listen to the words of Neco from the mouth of God.” Two crucial terms: wayyithappēś (disguised himself) — the same verb used of Ahab at Ramoth-gilead (2 Chronicles 18:29), connecting Josiah’s death to Ahab’s in a tragic typological echo. And mippî ʾĕlōhîm (from the mouth of God) — the Chronicler’s stunning verdict that Neco was YHWH’s instrument, a divine word Josiah refused.
23: Archers shoot King Josiah — wayyōrû hayōrîm lammelek Yōšiyyāhû — and the king says to his servants: haʿăbîrûnî kî hāḥōlatî mᵉʾōḏ — “Take me away, for I am badly wounded.” The wounding at Megiddo with an arrow — as Ahab was wounded at Ramoth-gilead (2 Chronicles 18:33) — seals the parallel. The great reforming king dies as the apostate northern king died: arrow-wounded in battle, taken out of the chariot. The Chronicler does not moralize; he presents the parallel and trusts the reader to feel its weight.
24: Josiah’s servants take him out of the chariot (wayyaʿăbîrūhû min-hammerkaḇāh), put him in the second chariot, bring him to Jerusalem — and he dies. He is buried in the tombs of his fathers. All Judah and Jerusalem mourned for Josiah (wayyitʾabbᵉlû ʿālāyw kol-Yᵉhûḏāh wîrûšālaim). The national mourning for the death of the greatest reforming king since Hezekiah is the Chronicler’s measure of Josiah’s stature: his death is felt as a national catastrophe, a collective wound.
25: Jeremiah also lamented (wayyqônēn) for Josiah, and all the singing men and singing women spoke of Josiah in their laments to this day — wayyitᵉnûm lᵉḥōq ʿal-Yiśrāʾēl — these were made a rule/statute in Israel and are written in the Laments. Wayyqônēn is the technical term for the lament-genre (the qînāh) — the same genre as the book of Lamentations. Jeremiah’s lament for Josiah established the qînāh as a liturgical form; the lament that ends the reform era becomes the template for the laments that survive the exile.
26: The rest of the acts of Josiah, and his good deeds (ḥăsādāyw — his loyal acts), according to what is written in the Law of YHWH. Ḥăsādāyw — the Chronicler uses the covenant-love vocabulary (ḥesed) to characterize Josiah’s royal deeds. The king whose deeds are ḥesed-acts — loyal covenant love expressed in reform and Passover — is being evaluated by the highest covenantal standard. His life is a ḥesed-life.
27: His acts, first and last, are written in the book of the kings of Israel and Judah. The dual source citation — a single work encompassing both kingdoms — is the Chronicler’s bibliography for Josiah’s reign. The unified source reflects the Chronicler’s pan-Israelite perspective: Josiah’s reforms extended to the north (2 Chronicles 34:6-7); his Passover included “all Israel” (2 Chronicles 35:3). The last reforming king of the Davidic line is recorded in the book of all Israel.
